citation: Connolly JG, Nathanson JT, Sobotka S, Haider S, Gometz A, Lovell M, Choudhri T. Effect of Playing and Training at Altitude on Concussion Incidence in Professional Football. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(12):2325967118794928. DOI: 10.1177/2325967118794928
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study examined whether the protective effects of altitude on NFL concussion incidence are due to acute exposure (the 'slosh effect') or chronic adaptations to living and training at altitude. Data from 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (1920 team exposures, 569 concussions) were subdivided into four groups based on whether teams trained and played at high altitude (≥644 feet) or low altitude. Teams that trained AND played at high altitude (high-high group) had a 28% lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) than the overall rate (0.29/exposure), while teams from low altitude playing at high altitude showed no significant reduction.
tldr: NFL teams that both train and play at altitude above 644 feet have significantly fewer concussions than all other groups—a 28% reduction versus the league average. This protective effect appears to require chronic altitude adaptation, not just acute exposure, since low-altitude teams visiting high-altitude venues showed no benefit. Conversely, high-altitude teams traveling to play at low altitude had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure), suggesting the protection is quickly lost when players descend. For football programs, this data challenges the simple 'slosh effect' hypothesis and implies that long-term physiological adaptation—not just the game environment—is the key variable. Away teams also had significantly more concussions than home teams regardless of altitude.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available data from Frontline's Concussion Watch covering 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (weeks 1–16). A total of 1920 team exposures across 960 games were analyzed. Teams were categorized into four altitude groups: low-altitude training/playing below 644 ft (low-low), low-altitude training/playing above 644 ft (low-high), high-altitude training/playing below 644 ft (high-low), and high-altitude training/playing above 644 ft (high-high). Stadium elevations were approximated via web-based tools. Statistics included Spearman rho, Wilcoxon signed rank, unpaired t-tests, and one-way and two-way ANOVA.
findings: Total of 569 concussions across 1920 team exposures (overall rate 0.29/exposure). Away teams had significantly higher concussion rates than home teams (0.32 vs. 0.27/exposure; P=.03). The high-high group had a significantly lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) compared with low-low (0.31; P<.05), high-low (0.36; P<.05), and overall (P<.05). The low-high group (0.32/exposure) was not significantly different from other groups. No dose-response relationship between altitude and concussion rate was found (R²=0.005). Quartile analysis showed no significant differences across altitude tiers.
limitations: Use of the binary 644-foot threshold may oversimplify a physiological spectrum; teams near this threshold (e.g., Green Bay at 640 ft vs. Indianapolis at 714 ft) may experience negligible differences. Altitudes studied are below the 2000-foot threshold where most documented physiological adaptations occur. Preseason altitude training was not accounted for. Data sourced from a public aggregator subject to underreporting. Small sample of high-altitude NFL teams (n=9 stadiums) limits statistical precision.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs based at altitude may carry an intrinsic concussion-risk advantage that diminishes when teams travel to lower elevations. This finding does not suggest teams should artificially simulate altitude but highlights environmental factors that may influence head injury protocols. Medical and performance staffs at high-altitude programs should be aware that this protective effect appears to dissipate rapidly upon descending, which may have implications for travel scheduling and pregame concussion baselines for away games. Low-altitude programs playing at high-altitude venues should not expect acute protection.
athleteDev: The altitude-concussion relationship is relevant primarily for programs located at elevation (e.g., Denver, Colorado Springs, Salt Lake City). For athlete development, the chronic physiological adaptations to altitude—including erythropoietic, vascular, and potentially neuroprotective changes—may have broader implications for training quality and recovery. Programs at low altitude would not be expected to gain concussion-risk benefits from short altitude training camps based on these data.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play protocols, but the finding that high-altitude teams playing away games had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure) raises the possibility that altitude descent could transiently increase concussion vulnerability. Clinicians managing RTP for players from altitude-based teams should consider whether rapid environmental changes after travel could influence concussion symptom presentation or risk of re-injury in the short term.

citation: Connolly JG, Nathanson JT, Sobotka S, Haider S, Gometz A, Lovell M, Choudhri T. Effect of Playing and Training at Altitude on Concussion Incidence in Professional Football. Orthop J Sports Med. 2018;6(12):2325967118794928. DOI: 10.1177/2325967118794928
abstract: NOTE: This entry (ID 175) is the same paper as ID 174 — identical content, authors, journal, and DOI. The two files differ only in filename casing and spacing (ID 174 uses lowercase with a double space before 'altitude'; ID 175 uses title case with single spaces). This descriptive epidemiology study examined whether the protective effects of altitude on NFL concussion incidence are due to acute exposure or chronic adaptations. Data from 2012–2015 NFL seasons showed teams that trained AND played at high altitude (≥644 ft) had a 28% lower concussion rate than the league average.
tldr: This is a duplicate file of ID 174 with a different filename casing. NFL teams that both train and play at altitude above 644 feet have significantly fewer concussions (28% reduction vs. league average). The protection requires chronic altitude adaptation—not acute exposure—since low-altitude teams visiting high-altitude venues gained no benefit. High-altitude teams playing away at low-altitude venues had the highest concussion rates, suggesting protection disappears rapidly upon descent. Away teams had more concussions than home teams regardless of altitude.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available data from Frontline's Concussion Watch covering 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons (weeks 1–16). A total of 1920 team exposures across 960 games were analyzed. Teams were categorized into four altitude groups based on training and game venue elevation relative to a 644-foot threshold. Stadium elevations were approximated via web-based tools. Statistics included Spearman rho, Wilcoxon signed rank, unpaired t-tests, and one-way and two-way ANOVA.
findings: Total of 569 concussions across 1920 team exposures (overall rate 0.29/exposure). Away teams had significantly higher concussion rates than home teams (0.32 vs. 0.27/exposure; P=.03). High-altitude teams playing at high altitude had a significantly lower concussion rate (0.20/exposure) compared with low-low (0.31; P<.05), high-low (0.36; P<.05), and overall (P<.05). Low-high teams (0.32/exposure) showed no significant difference from other groups. No dose-response relationship between stadium altitude and concussion rate was found (R²=0.005).
limitations: Use of the binary 644-foot threshold may oversimplify a physiological spectrum. Altitudes studied are below the 2000-foot threshold where most documented physiological adaptations occur. Preseason altitude training not accounted for. Data from a public aggregator subject to underreporting. Small sample of high-altitude NFL teams (n=9 stadiums).
practicalImplications: Same as ID 174: NFL and college football programs based at altitude may have an intrinsic concussion-risk advantage that diminishes when teams travel to lower elevations. Medical and performance staffs at high-altitude programs should note that protection appears to dissipate rapidly upon descent. Low-altitude programs visiting high-altitude venues should not expect acute concussion protection for their players.
athleteDev: Same as ID 174: Chronic altitude adaptation—not short-term exposure—appears to drive the concussion-protective effect. For athlete development, programs at elevation may benefit from related physiological adaptations (erythropoietic, vascular) that could support training quality and recovery. Short altitude training camps are unlikely to replicate these benefits based on current data.
rtp: Same as ID 174: This study does not directly address RTP protocols, but the finding that high-altitude teams playing away games had the highest concussion rate (0.36/exposure) suggests altitude descent could transiently increase concussion vulnerability. Clinicians managing RTP for altitude-based players should consider whether rapid environmental changes after travel could influence concussion symptom presentation or re-injury risk.

citation: Timmins RG, Bourne MN, Hickey JT, Maniar N, Tofari PJ, Williams MD, Opar DA. Effect of Prior Injury on Changes to Biceps Femoris Architecture across an Australian Football League Season. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2017;49(10):2102–2109. DOI: 10.1249/MSS.0000000000001333
abstract: This observational cohort study examined in-season changes in biceps femoris long head (BFlh) fascicle length in 30 elite Australian Football League players—12 with a prior unilateral hamstring strain injury (HSI) and 18 controls without HSI history. BFlh architecture was assessed via 2D ultrasound six times across the 23-week 2016 AFL season. Previously injured limbs had shorter fascicles than controls at all time points throughout the season. All groups showed an early-season increase in fascicle length followed by progressive shortening, with athletes who had prior HSI ending the season with shorter fascicles than they started with.
tldr: AFL players with a prior hamstring strain injury end the season with shorter biceps femoris fascicles than they began with—in both the previously injured and the contralateral uninjured limb. Players without injury history maintain fascicle length across the full season. All athletes see a brief early-season fascicle lengthening (peaking around week 5), but the subsequent shortening is more severe and sustained in previously injured athletes. The previously injured limb was shorter than controls at every measurement point throughout the year. This suggests that prior hamstring injury impairs structural adaptation across a full competitive season, likely due to residual neuromuscular inhibition, and that injury history must be a central consideration in any in-season hamstring management program.
methods: Observational retrospective cohort study during the 2016 AFL season (23 competitive weeks). Thirty elite male AFL players from two clubs participated: 18 controls (no HSI history) and 12 with a prior unilateral BFlh strain in the preceding 12 months. BFlh muscle thickness, pennation angle, and fascicle length were assessed via 2D B-mode ultrasound (12 MHz, GE Vivid-i) at weeks 1, 5, 9, 13, 17, and 23. Fascicle length was estimated using the validated Blazevich equation. Log-transformed data and Cohen's d effect sizes with 90% CIs were used; effects ≥0.2 = small, ≥0.5 = moderate, ≥0.8 = large.
findings: Previously injured limbs had shorter fascicles than controls at all time points (moderate to large effects; d range: −1.15 to −0.77). All groups peaked in fascicle length at week 5. The control group's fascicle length was similar at season start and end (trivial change). Previously injured limbs ended the season shorter than they started (moderate effect; d=−0.75). Contralateral uninjured limbs also ended shorter than at week 1 (small to moderate effects). The contralateral uninjured limb had shorter fascicles than controls from weeks 9–23 (moderate to large effects; d range: −0.87 to −0.54). Fascicle length peaked at week 5 across all groups then progressively shortened through week 23.
limitations: No preseason architecture data were collected (November–February), so fascicle changes during that period are unknown. No concurrent collection of match/training load, internal/external load, or resistance training variables. Two-dimensional ultrasound requires fascicle length estimation since full fascicles exceed the probe field of view (typical error ~0.30 cm). Three athletes sustained new HSIs during the study, requiring removal from later time points. Relatively small sample (n=30) from two clubs.
practicalImplications: For football programs, this study reinforces that prior hamstring injury is a chronic risk factor that persists structurally throughout an entire season—not just in the immediate post-injury period. Staff should use in-season ultrasound screening to identify players with short BFlh fascicles (particularly those with prior HSI) as candidates for targeted eccentric loading. The early in-season window (weeks 1–5) appears to be the period most responsive to fascicle lengthening interventions. After week 5, fascicles progressively shorten through the remainder of the season, making in-season eccentric hamstring maintenance critical.
athleteDev: Athletes with a prior hamstring strain show blunted structural adaptation to season demands compared to uninjured peers—a finding consistent with residual neuromuscular inhibition impairing the eccentric stimulus needed to add sarcomeres in series. Eccentric loading programs (e.g., Nordic hamstring curl, hip extension exercises) during the early in-season period and throughout the off-season are important tools for restoring BFlh fascicle length in previously injured athletes. Coaches should integrate systematic hamstring architecture monitoring into annual screening, recognizing that the contralateral 'uninjured' limb also shows reduced adaptation following unilateral HSI.
rtp: This study highlights that returning a player to play after hamstring strain injury does not reset the structural deficits in BFlh fascicle length. The previously injured limb maintained shorter fascicles than controls at every in-season assessment point, even when the athlete was cleared for full participation. RTP decisions should incorporate fascicle length assessment via ultrasound alongside traditional strength and functional criteria. Athletes with persistently short fascicles post-RTP should be considered for continued eccentric loading prescription and heightened hamstring strain recurrence monitoring throughout the season.

citation: Medeiros TM, Ribeiro-Alvares JB, Fritsch CG, Oliveira GS, Severo-Silveira L, Pappas E, Baroni BM. Effect of Weekly Training Frequency With the Nordic Hamstring Exercise on Muscle-Strain Risk Factors in Football Players: A Randomized Trial. Int J Sports Physiol Perform. 2020; (Ahead of Print). DOI: 10.1123/ijspp.2018-0780
abstract: This randomized trial compared the effects of performing the Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) once versus twice per week across an 8-week program on hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk factors in 32 high-level football (soccer) players aged 18–23 years. Isokinetic strength (knee flexor/extensor peak torques, H:Q ratios) and BFlh muscle architecture (thickness, pennation angle, fascicle length) were assessed before and after the programs. Only the twice-weekly group demonstrated significant improvements in hamstring eccentric and concentric strength and H:Q ratios, while both groups achieved similar increases in BFlh muscle thickness and fascicle length.
tldr: For high-level football players, two NHE sessions per week are needed to achieve hamstring strength gains, whereas one session per week is sufficient to increase biceps femoris fascicle length and muscle thickness. The twice-weekly group improved hamstring eccentric peak torque by ~11% and concentric torque significantly, while the once-weekly group showed no isokinetic strength change. Both groups increased BFlh fascicle length by ~8–9% (~1 cm). This has direct practical value: during congested match schedules when two weekly NHE sessions are not feasible, a single session can still preserve or enhance muscle architecture—a modifiable HSI risk factor. However, for full hamstring strengthening, two weekly sessions should be the minimum target.
methods: Randomized trial with 32 male high-level football players (under-20 and under-23 from a national premier division club; ages 18–23 years) completing an 8-week NHE program. Group 1 (G1, n=15) performed NHE once per week; Group 2 (G2, n=17) performed NHE twice per week. Both groups followed the same progressive periodization (sets/reps per session). Isokinetic knee flexor/extensor strength (Biodex, 60°/s) and BFlh architecture (B-mode ultrasound, GE Vivid-i, 13 MHz) were assessed pre- and post-training. Analysis of covariance with baseline as covariate, independent t-tests for percentage changes, and Cohen's d effect sizes were used.
findings: G2 had significantly higher hamstring concentric peak torque post-training than G1 (155–164 vs. 149–158 N·m; P=.043; ES=0.27). Trends favored G2 for hamstring eccentric peak torque (212–234 vs. 198–221 N·m; P=.098; ES=0.37), H:Q conventional ratio (P=.089; ES=0.31), and H:Q functional ratio (P=.076; ES=0.50). G2 showed significantly larger percentage changes in hamstring eccentric and concentric torque and H:Q functional ratio (P≤.028; large ESs). No between-group differences were found for BFlh thickness (P=.864), pennation angle (P=.289), or fascicle length (P=.406). Both groups increased BFlh thickness (small-moderate ESs) and fascicle length (~8–9%; small ESs) within-group.
limitations: No control group (without NHE) due to club requirements; prior evidence suggests outcomes would remain unchanged in a non-NHE group. High dropout rate due to in-season injuries (15 of 54 excluded), reducing sample size. Baseline strength values differed between groups by up to 8% in some measures, potentially giving G2 greater room for improvement despite statistical covariate adjustment. Only left limb was assessed for muscle architecture. Compliance was 100% by study design (minimum thresholds applied), which may not reflect real-world adherence.
practicalImplications: Football coaching and medical staff should prioritize at least two NHE sessions per week to develop meaningful hamstring strength adaptations in high-level players. During match-congested periods where two sessions are impractical, a single weekly NHE session can still meaningfully increase BFlh fascicle length—preserving an important structural risk factor. This pragmatic dose-response finding helps staff make evidence-based decisions about NHE frequency when full evidence-based protocols (3x/week for 10 weeks) are not feasible. Programs should view the NHE as a year-round tool rather than a preseason-only intervention.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, this study clarifies the dose of NHE needed to drive different adaptations: muscle architecture (fascicle length, thickness) responds to even low weekly volumes, while strength gains require higher frequency. Given that short BFlh fascicles and eccentric hamstring weakness are independent predictors of future HSI, programs should design NHE progressions that achieve both targets. For younger or developing players who may have lower baseline strength, the twice-weekly protocol is especially important to build the strength reserve needed to withstand high-speed running demands.
rtp: During rehabilitation and return to play after hamstring strain injury, this study suggests that even a once-weekly NHE may be sufficient to begin restoring BFlh fascicle length—a key structural adaptation for reinjury prevention. However, achieving meaningful eccentric strength recovery prior to clearance for full training and competition requires at least twice-weekly NHE sessions. RTP criteria should include both fascicle length normalization (via ultrasound) and eccentric strength symmetry, with NHE frequency titrated accordingly as the athlete progresses through the rehabilitation continuum.

citation: Gillen ZM, Burch RF, Saucier DN, Strawderman L, Luczak T, Piroli A, Petway AJ, Rath T. Effects of a Strength and Conditioning Offseason Program on Countermovement Jump Ground Reaction Forces in Division I American Football Players. J Strength Cond Res. 2023;XX(X):1–10. DOI: 10.1519/JSC.0000000000004600
abstract: This pre-post repeated-measures study examined the effects of a 10-week NCAA Division I football offseason strength and conditioning program on ground reaction forces (GRFs) during bilateral countermovement jumps (CMJ) and single-leg jumps (SLJ) in 16 players. Force plates quantified unweighting, braking, propulsive, and performance metrics, along with a jump efficiency index. CMJ propulsive phase duration decreased while peak braking power, peak propulsive power, mean propulsive force, and jump height increased. SLJ improvements included peak braking power, force at the low position, braking RFD, eccentric force, peak propulsive power, mean propulsive force, and jump height in both legs. Jump efficiency improved for both CMJ and SLJ.
tldr: Ten weeks of an offseason S&C program significantly improved vertical jump performance in Division I football players across multiple GRF metrics for both bilateral and unilateral jumps. Key findings: CMJ jump height increased (~5%), propulsive phase duration decreased (indicating more explosive force application), and the CMJ efficiency index improved (force at the low position closer to peak force). Single-leg jumps showed even broader improvements, including braking RFD, eccentric force, and propulsive power for both legs. This study is the first to document pre-post GRF changes from an offseason program in this population, providing a detailed framework for how S&C professionals can use force plate data to quantify program effectiveness—beyond simple jump height—and monitor eccentric and concentric adaptations that transfer to sprint, change-of-direction, and injury-resilience outcomes on the field.
methods: Pre-post repeated-measures design with 16 NCAA Division I American football players (height 187.01±3.05 cm; body mass 102.33±9.33 kg) who completed ≥90% of a 10-week offseason S&C program (resistance training, plyometrics, speed/agility, anaerobic conditioning; 4 sessions/week with a deload in week 6). Players performed 3 CMJ and 3 SLJ (each leg) attempts on a single AMTI AccuGait force plate (1 kHz) at pre- and post-testing. GRFs were processed offline in LabVIEW to calculate unweighting, braking, propulsive, and performance metrics including a jump efficiency index. Dependent samples t-tests (CMJ) and two-way repeated-measures ANOVA (leg × time for SLJ) were used; alpha=0.05.
findings: Body mass increased significantly (p=0.043; d=0.553). CMJ: peak braking power increased (p=0.023; d=0.545), propulsive phase duration decreased (p=0.007; d=0.691), peak propulsive power increased (p<0.001; d=1.201), mean propulsive force increased (p=0.012; d=0.630), and jump height increased (p=0.005; d=0.727). CMJ efficiency index improved (p=0.016; d=0.676; 0.07→0.04). SLJ (both legs): peak braking power (p=0.044; η²=0.245), force at low position (p<0.001; η²=0.662), braking RFD (p<0.001; η²=0.587), eccentric force (p=0.030; η²=0.276), peak propulsive power (p=0.001; η²=0.555), mean propulsive force (p=0.026; η²=0.288), and jump height (p=0.002; η²=0.469) all increased. SLJ efficiency index improved (p=0.001; η²=0.552). No significant leg × time interactions were found.
limitations: Small sample size (n=16) limits position-specific analyses. No control group without S&C training. Jump efficiency index is relatively understudied; reliability data are not yet established and individual responses were variable (5 subjects did not improve CMJ efficiency). Sprint and change-of-direction performance were not concurrently assessed to confirm transfer of jump improvements. Body mass increased, which may confound absolute force interpretations for some metrics. The specific contribution of individual program components (resistance training vs. plyometrics vs. conditioning) cannot be isolated.
practicalImplications: This study validates the use of bilateral and unilateral force plate CMJ assessments as sensitive tools for monitoring offseason S&C program effectiveness in collegiate football players. Strength and conditioning coaches should track GRF metrics—particularly braking phase variables, propulsive power, and the efficiency index—not just jump height, as these provide mechanistic insight into eccentric and concentric adaptations. SLJ assessments appear especially sensitive to training-induced braking and eccentric force changes and can also inform injury risk monitoring. A 10-week offseason block is sufficient to produce meaningful, statistically significant improvements across multiple GRF metrics, supporting its use as a standard assessment window.
athleteDev: The improvements in eccentric (braking) and concentric (propulsive) GRF metrics after 10 weeks reflect enhanced stretch-shortening cycle efficiency, which is foundational for sprint acceleration, cutting, and jumping performance in football. The jump efficiency index—measuring how close force at the low position is to peak force—improved from 7% to 4% difference for the CMJ, suggesting athletes better compressed the 'spring' of the countermovement. For linemen and skill positions alike, tracking these changes across offseason blocks can guide individualized programming decisions and help coaches identify athletes with 'energy leakage' during the eccentric phase that may limit explosive performance.
rtp: The SLJ has been identified in the literature as a potential indicator of knee injury risk and return-to-sport status after ACL reconstruction (Kotsifaki et al., 2022, cited within). This study demonstrates that SLJ GRF metrics improve meaningfully after an offseason program, establishing training-response benchmarks relevant to return-to-play progressions. For athletes recovering from knee injuries—the most common injury type in American football—regular force plate SLJ assessments can help sports medicine staff objectively track recovery of eccentric and propulsive force capabilities and determine readiness for return to full practice and competition.
